title: About me
text: I am a super crafty person! My grandma taught me to sew and crochet at a young age, and I never stopped. I make clothing items for myself from scratch, upcycle items that I thrift, and tailor clothes for my friends. I also enjoy embroidery, painting, and coloring. Check out these bowls I just made at a pottery studio in Goleta! It was my first time wheel-throwing.

title: About me
text:
My love for tea
I am a self-proclaimed tea connoisseur. English breakfast and earl grey are my morning go-tos, and I default to matcha lattes in the afternoons. I like to experiment with making my own syrups and trying different flavor combinations. My current favorite is a passionfruit vanilla matcha latte. At any given time, chances are I’ll be holding a fun drink!

title: About me
text:
Travels

























Last summer, I was fortunate to have the opportunity to study abroad in Barcelona, Spain. I took classes in Spanish during the week and explored the region on the weekends. I especially enjoyed a trip to Venice, Italy, where I then took the train to Lake Garda. See the interactive map below for my other past destinations!

title: ENV S 116 Final Project: Disproportionality
text:
Disproportionality.


I created a tapestry that depicts the concept of disproportionality and its roots in the social inequalities that our systems are built on. On the left side representing the privileged few, two people stand on a pyramid of money. They have a healthy, bright blue river, a living tree, and shrubs, since the pollution they create and profit from is affecting the other side of the land. They can look up and see stars in the night sky. Their pollution barely degrades their part of the world, so they don’t feel a need to change how they live. Their high-polluting buildings and factories are purposefully placed as close as possible to neighborhoods of low socioeconomic status. On the right side depicting the majority of the population, their sky of stars is replaced with a cloud of gray. Instead of a pyramid of money, the people have built scaffolding to bring them up to the same height as the privileged few. I used a dark red color for the ground to provoke feelings of urgency and doom from the viewer. I wanted the megaphone to stand out and represent urgency, so it is the only part of the tapestry in white and bright red. The viewer interprets whether the privileged few will choose to listen and change their ways or continue to turn a blind eye to the majority of the population’s suffering.
In the spirit of sustainability, my tapestry is made completely of secondhand or scrap materials I have collected or been donated over my time as a seamstress, with a significant portion being both secondhand and scrap material. Even the thread is secondhand! When I create, whether that be with sewing or other methods, I do it as sustainably as I can. In my mind, sewing with secondhand fabric makes the process even more special, as the fabric itself has a story behind it.
My tapestry was later featured in a special issue of a virtual magazine and in an environmental art gallery in the Isla Vista Community Center.

title: Things I’ve made
text: After a day of staring at a screen, I like to mix it up with a hands-on activity.

title: Things I’ve made
text:
Baking
This past year I’ve been experimenting with yeast! I baked my first loaf of bread and tested out cinnamon rolls and cardamom buns. I’m building up courage to tackle sourdough next year.






One of my recent loaves







Cinnamon rolls with extra cinnamon







Cardamom buns inspired by my summer in Barcelona

title: Things I’ve made
text:
Sewing
A recent project I am proud of is the dress I made for my trip to Ibiza, Spain. I created the pattern from scratch and used secondhand fabric that was passed down to me. Occasionally I sew by hand, like when I used a blanket stitch to add elbow patches to my dad’s favorite shirt.


















In middle and high school, I was part of a local entrepreneurship program for young women. Here was one of my table spreads at a pop-up shop! I still enjoy making zip bags and potholders for friends and family as gifts.

title: Things I’ve made
text:
Crochet
Cat hats are a quick project that require little time and produce a lot of joy (not their joy, as you can see).

title: How to Generate Load Profiles with ResStock and ComStock
text:
ResStock time series graph example.


In January 2026, I began interning as as a Programs Associate at the Clean Coalition. My work includes conducting site-specific Solar Microgrid Feasibility Studies and collaborating with a variety of organizations to accelerate the transition to renewable energy.
My first blog post “How to Generate Load Profiles with ResStock and ComStock” describes the process of using the residential and commercial building model databases, ResStock and ComStock, to complete the Load Profiles portion of the Clean Coalition’s Solar Microgrid Methodology (SMM).

title: ENV S 193DS: Homework 2
text: This is Problem 2 of my Homework 2 assignment, which is attached at the bottom of the page. In this problem, I used statistical testing to determine whether consumption of nutrient-enriched seaweed leads to increases in urchin gonad weight. Red sea urchin gonads are of high value in California’s fishing economy, so cultivating urchins with larger gonads would bring in more income.

title: ENV S 193DS: Homework 2
text:
Read in packages and data


Code
# loading in packages
library("tidyverse")
library("janitor")
library("effsize")

# creating new object called urchins, storing data
urchins <- read_csv("urchin-experiment-data.csv")

title: ENV S 193DS: Homework 2
section: a. Clean the urchin weight data
text:
a. Clean the urchin weight data
Clean column names, select for preferred columns, and display 10 random rows from the data frame.


Code
# create new object from urchins 
urchins_clean <- urchins |> 
 # clean column names
   clean_names() |> 
  drop_na() |> 
  # change c and n to be full words 
  mutate(experimental_treatment = case_when( 
    # fill in Control to replace c
    experimental_treatment == "c" ~ "Control", 
    # fill in Nutrient-enriched to replace n
    experimental_treatment == "n" ~ "Nutrient-enriched" 
  )) |> 
  # select experimental_treatment and gonad weight columns
  select(experimental_treatment, weight_g)

# display 10 random rows 
slice_sample(urchins_clean, n = 10)


# A tibble: 10 × 2
   experimental_treatment weight_g
   <chr>                     <dbl>
 1 Control                    35.2
 2 Nutrient-enriched          33.3
 3 Nutrient-enriched          28  
 4 Nutrient-enriched          29.5
 5 Nutrient-enriched          33.8
 6 Nutrient-enriched          26.8
 7 Control                    30.9
 8 Control                    31.9
 9 Control                    34.2
10 Control                    25

title: ENV S 193DS: Homework 2
section: b. Create a summary of the data
text:
b. Create a summary of the data


Code
# create new object from urchins_clean
urchins_summary <- urchins_clean |> 
  group_by(experimental_treatment) |> 
  summarize( # add what i want in summary
    mean = mean(weight_g),
    n = length(weight_g),
    sd = sd(weight_g))  |>
  ungroup() # ungroup the data frame

urchins_summary # display the summary


# A tibble: 2 × 4
  experimental_treatment  mean     n    sd
  <chr>                  <dbl> <int> <dbl>
1 Control                 26.8    42  8.67
2 Nutrient-enriched       31.7    35  3.91

title: ENV S 193DS: Homework 2
text:
c. Create an SD plot


Code
# start with the urchins_clean data frame
ggplot(data = urchins_clean, 
       # x-axis should be experimental treatment
       # y-axis should be urchin gonad weight 
       # coloring by experimental treatment
       mapping = aes(x = experimental_treatment, 
                     y = weight_g, 
                     color = experimental_treatment)) + 
   # first layer: jitterplot
   geom_jitter(height = 0, # no jitter in vertical direction
              width = 0.2, # small horizontal jitter
              shape = 21) + # open circle shape 
  # second layer: represent means at each site
  stat_summary(geom = "point",
               fun = mean,
               size = 3) + # make points bigger
  # third layer: bars to represent standard deviation
  geom_errorbar(data = urchins_summary, # use summary data frame
                mapping = aes(x = experimental_treatment, 
                              y = mean, # assign axes based on summary frame
                              ymin = mean - sd, 
                              ymax = mean + sd,
                width = 0.1)) + # make the bars narrower
  # relabelling x- and y-axes
  labs(x = "Experimental treatment", 
       y = "Urchin gonad weight (grams)") + 
  theme_light() + # custom theme
  # custom colors
  scale_color_manual(values = c("Control" = "darkblue",
                                "Nutrient-enriched" = "red3")) +
  theme(legend.position = "none") # taking out legend

title: ENV S 193DS: Homework 2
text:
d. Hypotheses
Null hypothesis: The difference in mean urchin gonad weight between urchins who consume normal seaweed and those who consume nutrient-enriched seaweed is equal to 0.
Alternative hypothesis: The difference in mean urchin gonad weight between urchins who consume normal seaweed and those who consume nutrient-enriched seaweed is not equal to 0.

title: ENV S 193DS: Homework 2
section: e. QQ plot to determine normality
text:
e. QQ plot to determine normality


Code
# base layer: ggplot 
ggplot(data = urchins_clean, # starting data frame
       aes(sample = weight_g)) + # y-axis for QQ plot 
  # first layer: QQ reference line 
  geom_qq_line(color = "red") + # adding a color
  # second layer: QQ plot
  geom_qq() + 
  # creating "facets"
  facet_wrap(~experimental_treatment) # show treatments separately

title: ENV S 193DS: Homework 2
section: f. Justify distribution based on QQ plot
text:
f. Justify distribution based on QQ plot
Urchin gonad weight is normally distributed because the data points follow a straight diagonal line with a 45 degree angle.

title: ENV S 193DS: Homework 2
section: g. Check variances with a “gut check” and a statistical check
text:
g. Check variances with a “gut check” and a statistical check


Code
# calculate variances with gut check
urchin_weight_var <- urchins_clean |> # starting data frame
  group_by(experimental_treatment) |> # group by experimental treatment
  summarize(variance = var(weight_g)) # calculate variances

# calculate variance ratio 
urchin_weight_var


# A tibble: 2 × 2
  experimental_treatment variance
  <chr>                     <dbl>
1 Control                    75.2
2 Nutrient-enriched          15.3


Code
75.2/15.3 # gut check


[1] 4.915033


Code
# statistical check: F test of equal variances
var.test(
  weight_g ~ experimental_treatment, # formula: response variable ~ grouping variable
  data = urchins_clean) # data: urchins-clean data frame



    F test to compare two variances

data:  weight_g by experimental_treatment
F = 4.9054, num df = 41, denom df = 34, p-value = 7.185e-06
alternative hypothesis: true ratio of variances is not equal to 1
95 percent confidence interval:
 2.527793 9.330193
sample estimates:
ratio of variances 
          4.905405

title: ENV S 193DS: Homework 2
text:
h. Do a t-test


Code
t.test( 
  # formula: response variable ~ grouping variable
  weight_g ~ experimental_treatment, 
  # argument for unequal variances
  var.equal = FALSE, 
  # data: urchins_clean data frame
  data = urchins_clean)



    Welch Two Sample t-test

data:  weight_g by experimental_treatment
t = -3.2739, df = 59.238, p-value = 0.001774
alternative hypothesis: true difference in means between group Control and group Nutrient-enriched is not equal to 0
95 percent confidence interval:
 -7.873119 -1.900214
sample estimates:
          mean in group Control mean in group Nutrient-enriched 
                       26.77619                        31.66286

title: ENV S 193DS: Homework 2
text:
i. Explanation of test choice
A t-test would have been appropriate for testing my hypothesis from part d because I am testing for a true difference in means between two groups. I evaluated normality by creating a QQ plot and evaluated homogeneity of variance by manually calculating the variances and running an F test. Because the group variances were unequal, I decided to do a Welch’s Two Sample t-test.

title: ENV S 193DS: Homework 2
text:
j. Calculate effect size


Code
# calculate cohen's d 
d <- cohen.d(weight_g ~ experimental_treatment, data = urchins_clean)

d # display effect size value 



Cohen's d

d estimate: -0.7049911 (medium)
95 percent confidence interval:
     lower      upper 
-1.1747569 -0.2352252

title: ENV S 193DS: Homework 2
text:
k. Statistical summary
We found evidence to suggest that consumption of nutrient-enriched seaweed has a moderate effect (Cohen’s d = 0.70) on mean urchin gonad weights (Welch Two Sample t-test, t(59.2) = -3.27, p=0.002, \(\alpha\) = 0.05). On average, urchins who ate nutrient-enriched seaweed (n=35, mean=31.8) had higher gonad weights than urchins who ate normal seaweed (n=42, mean=26.7).

title: ENV S 193DS: Homework 2
text: Full assignment

title: Stella Symonds
text: Hi! My name is Stella Symonds and I am from Orinda, CA. I am in my third year at UCSB pursuing a B.S. in Environmental Studies with a concentration in Statistics. This website serves as a personal portfolio. Please reach out via email if you’d like to connect!
